title: Urashima Taro: The Fisherman and the Sea Kingdom
summary: A kind fisherman is rewarded with a visit to the underwater Dragon Palace, only to return to a world where centuries have passed in a heartbeat.
full_text:
Long ago, in the province of Tango, in the little fishing village of Mizu-no-ye, there lived a young fisherman named Urashima Taro. He was kind-hearted and skilled, able to catch more bonito and bream in a day than his companions could in a week. One afternoon, as he walked along the shore, he found a group of children tormenting a small turtle. They poked it with sticks and threw stones, laughing as the creature struggled toward the sea. "Stop!" Urashima cried, and he shooed the children away. He carried the turtle gently to the water's edge and released it.

The next day, as he fished from his boat, a great turtle surfaced beside him. "Urashima Taro," it said, "I am the turtle you saved. In gratitude, let me take you to the Palace of the Dragon King beneath the sea." Urashima, astonished, climbed onto the turtle's back. Down they dove, through waters of deepening blue, past coral reefs and schools of glittering fish, until they reached a palace of such beauty that Urashima could not speak. Its walls were of pearl, its roof of coral, its gardens of seaweed that swayed like dancers in an underwater breeze.

The princess Otohime came forth to greet him. She was more beautiful than any creature of the upper world, robed in silks that shimmered like sea foam. "I was the turtle you saved," she said. "Will you stay here with me, in the land of eternal youth? Marry me, and live forever without age or pain." Urashima was enchanted. Days passed in feasting and dancing. The fishes performed ballets, the dragons sang, and every pleasure imaginable was his. He forgot the world above, forgot his mother, forgot his village.

But at last, a shadow crossed his heart. "I must return home," he said. "My parents will be worried." Otohime wept, but she did not stop him. She gave him a small lacquer box tied with a silk cord. "This is the tamatebako," she said. "Never open it. As long as you keep it closed, you carry my love." Urashima returned to the surface, but as he stepped onto the beach, he found the village unrecognizable. The houses were different, the people were strangers, and the names of his parents were only distant memories in old books. He discovered that while he had spent only a few years in the Dragon Palace, three hundred years had passed on land. Everyone he had ever known was long dead.

Overwhelmed by grief and loneliness, Urashima forgot the princess's warning. In a moment of desperation, he untied the silk cord and opened the tamatebako. A plume of white smoke billowed out, and as it touched his skin, the three centuries of lost time caught up with him in a single heartbeat. His hair turned white, his skin wrinkled, and his strength vanished. In an instant, he became an old man, weathered by the weight of time. He sank to the sand, looking out at the ocean, realizing that the price of a momentary paradise was the loss of everything he had ever truly loved.
moral: Time is the most precious of all treasures; to ignore it is to lose the very essence of human connection.

source_references.title: Dawn to the West: Japanese Literature of the Modern Era
source_references.author: Donald Keene
source_references.note: Keene traces the Urashima Tarō legend from its origins in the Man'yōshū (759) through medieval literary and folk traditions.

title: The Ugly Duckling
summary: It was summertime, and the countryside was beautiful.. A mother duck was sitting on her nest, waiting for her eggs to hatch.
full_text:
It was summertime, and the countryside was beautiful. A mother duck was sitting on her nest, waiting for her eggs to hatch. At last, the eggs began to crack—one by one, fluffy yellow ducklings tumbled out. But one egg remained, larger than all the others.

Finally, the great big egg cracked, and out came a large, gray duckling. He was homely and awkward—nothing like his pretty yellow siblings. The mother duck looked at him with surprise, wondering if he could really be hers.

The next day, the mother duck took all her children to the farmyard. There was a pond, and the ducklings swam beautifully. But the gray duckling was left behind, and the farmyard was full of mean ducks who pecked at him. 'He is so ugly! they quacked. Even his own brothers and sisters were unkind to him.

The ugly duckling was so sad that he ran away. He wandered through the fields and meadows, lonely and cold. He came to a pond where wild ducks lived. 'You are very ugly, said the wild ducks, 'but as long as you dont bother our families, you may stay.' A hunters dog came close, but when he saw the ugly duckling, he thought he was not worth catching and ran away.

The ugly duckling wandered on through bushes and tall grass. An old woman found him and put him in her oven to warm him, but she shooed him out in a hurry. He wandered through a garden where roses bloomed, and a swallow asked him why he was so ugly. But the swallow was shot by a hunter, and the ugly duckling was left alone again.

Winter came, and the pond froze. The ugly duckling swam round and round to keep a patch of water open, but eventually he fell exhausted into the ice. A kind farmer found him, picked him up with his bare hands, and brought him home to his wife and children. The children wanted to play with him roughly, but the kind farmer saved him.

At last, spring arrived. The ugly duckling saw three beautiful white birds flying toward him—swans! They were so graceful, so beautiful. The other birds on the pond quacked merrily, and the children called out, 'Look! There are new birds—see how beautiful! The ugly duckling bowed his head, hoping they would not notice his ugliness.

But what did he see reflected in the water? Not an ugly gray duckling—he saw a beautiful white swan! The other swans came to him and stroked his feathers with their beaks. Children ran to call everyone: 'Come look! Three new swans—there is a new one, the most beautiful! They threw bread and cake into the water.

The ugly duckling was so happy he had been through so much and had never given up hope. Now he was beautiful, and the swans welcomed him. He felt such joy—he had never known such happiness.

And so he lived happily, floating on the beautiful pond, surrounded by the love and admiration he had always dreamed of.
moral: What seems ugly may become beautiful.

source_references.title: Hans Christian Andersen's Complete Fairy Tales
source_references.author: Erik Christian Haugaard (trans.)

title: The Urko of North Wij
summary: In the mountain of Urberg dwells the Urko a monster cow whose plowing created Lake Sommen. She is fettered by a horseshoe eating one hair from a cowhide each Christmas until the hairs run out and the world ends. If a king crosses Ydre she will break free having already killed King Frode.
full_text:
From the point where the river Bulsjö empties into Lake Sommen, extending in a northerly
direction for about eight miles, bordering the parishes of North Wij and Asby, nearly
up to a point called Hornäs, stretches the principal fjord, one of several branching
off from the large lake.

Near Vishult, in the first named of these parishes, descending to the lake from the
elevation that follows its west shores, is a wall-like precipice, Urberg, which, from
the lake, presents an especially magnificent view, as well in its height and length,
and in its wood-crowned top, as in the wild confusion of rocks at its base, where,
among the jumble of piled-up slabs of stones, gape large openings, into which only
the imagination dares to intrude.

From this point the mountain range extends southward toward Tulleram, and northward,
along the shore of Lake Sjöhult, under the name of Tjorgaberg, until it ends in an
agglomeration of rocks called Knut’s Den.

In this mountain dwells the Urko, a monster cow of traditionary massiveness, which,
in former times, when she was yet loose, plowed the earth, making what is now Lake
Sommen and its many fjords. At last she was captured and fettered by a Troll man from Tulleram, who squeezed a horseshoe
around the furious animal’s neck and confined her in Urberg. For food she has before
her a large cow-hide from which she may eat a hair each Christmas eve, but when all
the hairs are consumed, she will be liberated and the destruction of Ydre and all
the world is to follow.

But even before this she will be liberated from her prison if Ydre is crossed by a
king whom she follows and kills if she can catch him before he has crossed to the
confines of the territory.

It happened one time that a king named Frode, or Fluga, passed through Ydre, and,
conscious of the danger, hurried to reach the boundaries, but, believing he had already
passed them, he halted on the confines at Fruhammer, or, as the place was formerly
called, Flude, or Flugehammer, where he was overtaken and gored to death by the monster.
In confirmation of this incident, his grave, marked by four stones, is to this day
pointed out.

Another narrative, which, however, is known only in the southeastern part of the territory,
relates that another king, unconscious of the danger accompanying travel in the neighborhood,
passed unharmed over the border, and had reached the estate of Kalleberg, when he
heard behind him the dreadful bellowing of the monster in full chase after him. The
king hastened away as speedily as possible. The cow monster, unable to check its mad
gallop at the border, rushed over some distance to the place where the king first
paused, where, in the gravel-mixed field, she pawed up a round hole of several hundred feet in breadth, which became a bog, whose border, especially
upon the north side, is surrounded by a broad wall of the upheaved earth.

Still, at times, especially preceding a storm, the Urko is heard rattling its fetters
in the mountain, and both upon the mountain and down near the shore of the lake by
times.

Extraordinary things are said to happen. One and another of the residents thereabouts
assert even that they have seen the Urko in her magnificent rooms and halls, which
the neighbors do not for a moment doubt.
moral: Primordial forces bound in the earth await their release and kings themselves are not safe from their wrath.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1888), translated by W.H. Myers.

title: The Unwelcome Guest
summary: A story exploring the laws of hospitality and the consequences of betrayal. A guest arrives unexpectedly and proceeds to cause chaos and distress, testing the patience, virtue, and generosity of the hosts in a challenging social dynamic.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. A story exploring the laws of hospitality and the consequences of betrayal. A guest arrives unexpectedly and proceeds to cause chaos and distress, testing the patience, virtue, and generosity of the hosts in a challenging social dynamic. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

source_references.title: The Kalevala: An Epic Poem after the Oral Traditions of Finland
source_references.author: Elias Lönnrot (trans. Francis Peabody Magoun Jr.)

title: Uksáhkká: Guardian of the Door
summary: Uksáhkká (Door Woman) is one of the daughters of Máttaráhkká, living under the door of the goahti. She protects people entering and leaving the dwelling, receives the newborn child into the world during birth, and protects children as they learn to walk. Drinks were poured under the door as offerings. Christian influence equated her with Virgin Mary. She was known only among Scandinavian Sámi.
full_text: Uksáhkká dwells beneath the threshold of every goahti, in the invisible space between inside and outside. Her name means 'Door Woman,' and her domain is the boundary of the home. Whenever someone steps through the door, Uksáhkká watches over them, protecting them from harm as they pass from the safety of the hearth to the dangers of the wilderness. Her most important task comes during birth. When the child emerges from the mother, Sáráhkká passes it to Uksáhkká, who receives it into the world. She is the first divine being the newborn meets, the one who welcomes them to the middle world. As the child grows and learns to walk, Uksáhkká steadies their steps, preventing falls and keeping them from wandering too far. Offerings were made to her by pouring drinks under the door. Christian missionaries later told the Sámi that Uksáhkká was actually the Virgin Mary, but the old women knew the difference. Uksáhkká was known only among the Scandinavian Sámi of Norway and Sweden, not in Finland or Russia, her worship tied to the specific shape of the goahti door.
moral: The threshold between worlds is sacred, guarded by those who welcome and protect.

title: The Ulda's Old Mother
summary: A Sámi story about the matriarch of the Ulda. The underground people are rich, beautiful, and well-dressed, with reindeer of exceptional colors. All gold and silver under the earth belongs to them. They care for the bear in his winter den, feeding him invisibly. They sometimes swap their children for human babies. If you throw a metal object over a herd of Ulda reindeer that leave no tracks in snow, the herd becomes yours.
full_text: The Ulda's old mother is the matriarch of the hidden people, ancient and wise. Under her rule, the Ulda live in splendor beneath the fells. Their reindeer have coats of colors never seen above ground—silver, gold, deep blue like the inside of a glacier. All the gold and silver that miners seek belongs to the Ulda. When humans dig too deep, they disturb the Ulda's halls. The old mother cares for the bear during his winter hibernation, feeding him invisibly so that he wakes strong in the spring. But she can also be cruel. Ulda mothers sometimes swap their sickly children for healthy human babies, leaving changelings in cradles. A human child taken by the Ulda might become one of them forever. There is a way to gain from the Ulda: if you see a herd of reindeer that leave no tracks in the snow, those are Ulda reindeer. Throw a metal object over the herd, and the herd becomes yours. But the old mother will not forget, and she will find a way to balance the debt.
moral: The hidden people have their own rules and their own justice, and debts must always be repaid.

title: Unhcegila the Serpent Monster
summary: Unhcegila was a gigantic serpent monster who wreaked havoc upon the land, devouring anyone who stumbled upon her. A red crystal in the seventh spot on her head functioned as her heart and granted its bearer great power. In one version, a warrior from the Bear Clan, guided by a Weasel spirit, allowed himself to be devoured then cut his way out, freeing other victims. In another, two brothers—one blind—killed her with arrows from a medicine woman. A third version tells of Unhcegila emerging from primordial waters, only to be struck down by Wakinyan the Thunderbird whose lightning shattered her heart, scattering her bones across the Badlands.
source: Wikipedia / Native American Mythology A to Z

source_references.title: The Sacred Pipe: Black Elk's Account of the Seven Rites of the Oglala Sioux
source_references.author: Brown, Joseph Epes

title: Uktena: The Horned Serpent (Cherokee)
summary: Uktena is a massive horned serpent covered in shining scales, with a single blazing crystal in its forehead that grants supernatural vision. The first Uktena was once a man who attempted to kill the sun. He failed and was transformed into the monstrous serpent as punishment. Uktena dwells in deep rivers, lakes, and under mountains. Its gaze alone can paralyze or kill. Only the bravest medicine men and heroes dare to hunt Uktena, seeking the Ulûñsû'tî, the diamond-like crystal from its forehead, which grants great power to whoever possesses it. The most famous story involves a hunter who stalked an Uktena to its lair and, after a great battle, retrieved the crystal. The stone brought power but also danger, and was eventually hidden away in a sacred place.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Uktena

source_references.title: Myths and Legends of the Cherokee
source_references.author: Mooney, James
source_references.publisher: D. Appleton and Company, New York

title: Unkulunkulu: The First Man Who Broke from the Reeds
summary: In the beginning there was only darkness and Uthlanga, the primordial reed bed. The god Umvelinqangi created a seed that grew into a massive reed, and at the end of one reed grew a man named Unkulunkulu, the 'old old one.' He grew so large that he snapped off from the reed and walked across the land, where he found men and women sprouting from other reeds. He picked them and they became the first ancestors of all nations. He gathered fish and flung them into rivers, harvested birds and antelope, and plucked cattle for humans. He threw a ball of fire and a glowing stone into the sky, creating the Sun and Moon. He also sent the chameleon with the message that men should not die, but the slow chameleon was beaten by a fast lizard carrying the opposite message, and so death entered the world.

source_references.title: Olden Times in Zululand and Natal: History of the Zulu-Xosa-People
source_references.author: A.T. Bryant

title: Uekera: The Tree of Knowledge
summary: In Kiribati legend, Uekera is a sacred tree that reaches to the heavens, known as the tree of knowledge. It was planted in Buariki village in North Tarawa by the goddess Nei Tekanuea. According to tradition, Uekera's roots extend deep into the earth while its branches reach into the sky world, making it a bridge between the human and divine realms. The tree is said to possess great wisdom and those who approach it with respect can gain knowledge and insight. Uekera inspired the name of Kiribati's weekly newspaper, Te Uekera. The tree symbolizes the connection between the people of Kiribati, their land, and their ancestors, representing the transmission of cultural knowledge from generation to generation through oral tradition.

title: Umar Marvi — The Faithful Woman of Sindh
summary: Marvi was a beautiful peasant woman from the Thar Desert village of Malir in Sindh. The local ruler, Umar Soomro, became obsessed with her and had her kidnapped and brought to his palace. He offered her wealth, jewels, and queenship if she would marry him, but Marvi refused steadfastly, remaining faithful to her betrothed, a simple farmer. She endured years of captivity and torture, never wavering in her loyalty. Eventually, Umar Soomro was so impressed by her unwavering virtue that he released her. Marvi returned to her village and married her true love. The story, immortalized in Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai's Shah Jo Risalo, is one of the seven tragic romances of Sindhi folklore and is celebrated as a symbol of truth, loyalty, and the unbreakable connection to one's homeland.
source: Shah Jo Risalo by Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai (18th century), Sindhi folklore
moral: Love transcends death but society's cruelty destroys it

title: Udea and Her Seven Brothers
summary: A Libyan fairy tale collected by Hans Stumme in Tripoli. A woman with seven sons gives birth to a daughter, Udea. The seven brothers are driven away from home by a rival female spirit and set out into the world. The mother promises them a sign when Udea is born: a red flag for a daughter, a white one for a son. The rival spirit arranges for a white flag to be raised instead of red, tricking the brothers into thinking they have another brother. Years later, Udea grows up, learns of her brothers, and sets out to find them. She travels through dangerous territory and eventually locates them, but her sister-in-law becomes jealous and sets impossible tasks for her, including fetching a magical bird and finding a singing tree. With supernatural help, Udea completes each task. Eventually, the truth is revealed, the jealous sister-in-law is punished, and Udea is reunited with her family. The tale includes themes of sibling loyalty, jealousy, and the triumph of perseverance over supernatural obstacles.
source: Wikipedia - Udea and Her Seven Brothers; snip Lang - The Grey Fairy Book

source_references.title: A Treasury of African Folklore: The Oral Literature, Traditions, Myths, Legends, Epics, Tales, Recollections, Wisdom, Sayings, and Humor of Africa
source_references.author: Harold Courlander
